perturbation.original_text: Company further agrees to return Confidential Information to FNHA, if FNHA so directs, or to destroy Confidential Information once the RFP process completes or earlier of FNHA so directs.
perturbation.changed_text: Company is not obligated to return or destroy Confidential Information upon completion of the RFP process or at FNHA's direction, unless specifically required by a separate written agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the requirement to return or destroy confidential information, potentially conflicting with privacy laws and regulations that mandate data destruction under certain circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), Canada
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.5 requires organizations to destroy, erase, or anonymize personal information that is no longer required to fulfill the identified purposes. By stating that the company is not obligated to return or destroy confidential information, it could violate PIPEDA if the confidential information contains personal information and is no longer needed.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the company is 'not obligated to return or destroy Confidential Information,' directly contradicting the principle outlined in the Law Explanation, which refers to PIPEDA Principle 4.5, mandating organizations to destroy, erase, or anonymize personal information that is no longer required. The original text required the company to return or destroy confidential information. By eliminating this obligation, the changed text creates a legal contradiction, as it permits the company to retain personal information indefinitely, even if no longer needed, which violates PIPEDA as explained.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance w ith the laws of the Province of British Columbia , without regard to or application of choice -of-law rules or principles and the parties submit to the jurisdiction of BC courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen at the sole discretion of FNHA, without notice to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows FNHA to unilaterally choose the governing law, which could violate principles of fairness and due process, especially if the chosen jurisdiction lacks a reasonable connection to the agreement or the parties. It can also create an imbalance in bargaining power.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding unconscionability and fairness
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law principles relating to Unconscionability
perturbation.law_explanation: Allowing one party to unilaterally choose the governing law without the other party's consent or knowledge may render the agreement unconscionable, especially if the chosen law is significantly disadvantageous to the non-choosing party. Unconscionability is a defense to the enforcement of a contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: unconscionability | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you unconscionability Unconscionability is a defense against the enforcement of a contract or portion of a contract. If a contract is unfair or oppressive to one party in a way that suggests abuses during its formation, a court may find it unconscionable and refuse to enforce it. There are two types of unconscionability in contracts, procedural and substantive. Procedural unconscionability is present when during the contract’s formation, at least one party does not have fair/meaningful choice, there’s misrepresentation, or unequal bargaining power among other factors. Substantive unconscionability is when the contract’s terms unfairly benefit/harm one side such as extremely unequal price compared to value exchanged. A contract is most likely to be found unconscionable if both unfair bargaining and unfair substantive terms are shown. An absence of meaningful choice by the disadvantaged party is often used to prove unfair bargaining. For example, in J ones v Star Credit , the Court refused to enforce a contract that charged three times the market value of an appliance to a low-income plaintiff , with significantly unequal education and experience compared to the defendant , by finding the contract unconscionable. [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities consumer protection business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows FNHA to unilaterally choose the governing law without notice to the Company. This contradicts the principle of fair bargaining, potentially rendering the agreement unconscionable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unconscionability as a defense against contract enforcement if it's unfair or oppressive to one party due to unequal bargaining power, especially if the disadvantaged party lacks meaningful choice. By allowing FNHA sole discretion in choosing the governing law, the changed text introduces a significant imbalance, potentially depriving the Company of fair legal recourse, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company acknowledges that all of the Confidential Information is owned solely by FNHA and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain. Accordingly, Company agrees that FNHA will have the right to obtain an immediate injunction enjoining any breach of this Agreement, as well as the rig ht to pursue any and all other rights and remedies available at law o r in equity for such a breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Company acknowledges that all Confidential Information is owned solely by FNHA. FNHA will have the right to obtain an injunction enjoining any breach of this agreement. Company waives all rights to challenge the injunction, regardless of the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: This change forces the company to waive its right to challenge an injunction, which violates fundamental due process rights and the right to a fair hearing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Section 7
perturbation.law_citation: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Section 7
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms guarantees the right to life, liberty and security of the person and the right not to be deprived thereof except in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice. Waiving the right to challenge an injunction, regardless of circumstances, can violate principles of fundamental justice by denying a fair hearing and due process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Charterpedia - Section 7 – Life, liberty and security of the person Section 7 – Life, liberty and security of the person Previous Table of contents Next Provision 7. Everyone has the right to life, liberty and security of the person and the right not to be deprived thereof except in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice. Similar provisions Similar provisions may be found in the following Canadian laws and international instruments binding on Canada: section 1(a) of the Canadian Bill of Rights ; articles 6 and 9 of the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights ; articles 6 and 37 of the Convention on the Rights of the Child ; articles 14 and 17 of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities ; and article I of the American Declaration of the Rights and Duties of Man . See also the following international, regional and comparative law instruments that are not legally binding on Canada but include similar provisions: article 3 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights ; articles 4, 5 and 7 of the American Convention on Human Rights ; articles 2 and 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights ; the Fifth Amendment of the Constitution of the United States of America (the Due Process Clause). Purpose Section 7 of the Charter requires that laws or state actions that interfere with life, liberty and security of the person conform to the principles of fundamental justice â the basic principles that underlie our notions of justice and fair process ( Charkaoui v. Canada (Citizenship and Immigration) , [2007] 1 S.C.R. 350 at paragraph 19). Analysis Section 7 involves a two-step analysis: Is there a deprivation of one of the three (3) protected interests, that is to say life, liberty or security of the person? Is the deprivation in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice? This second step may be broken down into two steps, where it is necessary a) to identify the relevant principle or principles of fundamental justice and then b) to determine
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts fundamental justice as described in Section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, violating due process by forcing the company to waive its right to challenge an injunction, regardless of circumstances. The scraped law snippet 2, referencing Charterpedia on Section 7, states that everyone has the right to life, liberty, and security of the person and the right not to be deprived thereof except in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice. The modification in the contract explicitly removes a legal right, which falls under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company may not at any time directly or indirectly communicate with the media in relation to the Confidential Information or any information with respect to the RFP without first obtaining the written permission of FNHA.
perturbation.changed_text: Company may not, under any circumstance, communicate with any third party, including legal counsel, regarding the Confidential Information or the RFP without the express written consent of FNHA. Violation of this clause will result in immediate termination of any existing agreements and forfeiture of any payments due.
perturbation.explanation: This change restricts the company's ability to consult with legal counsel, which violates the right to seek legal advice, especially when facing potential legal issues related to the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Right to Counsel, general legal principle
perturbation.law_citation: General Legal Principle - Right to Counsel
perturbation.law_explanation: The right to counsel is a fundamental legal principle ensuring individuals can seek legal advice. Preventing communication with legal counsel, especially when facing potential legal issues, infringes upon this right and can render the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: right to counsel | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you right to counsel Overview The right to counsel refers to the right of a criminal defendant to have a lawyer assist in his defense, even if he cannot afford to pay for an attorney. The Sixth Amendment gives defendants the right to counsel in federal prosecutions. However, the right to counsel was not applied to state prosecutions for [wex:felony] offenses until 1963 in Gideon v. Wainwright , 372 U.S. 335. This was done through the incorporation doctrine . However, for certain misdemeanors , there is not a guaranteed right to counsel. When Does The Right to Counsel Attach? One area of controversy related to the right to counsel is the question of when the right attaches, or, in other words, when, in the process of criminal prosecution, the defendant gains the right to counsel. In Brewer v. Williams , 430 U.S. 387 (1977), the Supreme Court held that a defendant gains the right to an attorney “at or after the time that judicial proceedings have been initiated against him, whether by formal charge, preliminary hearing, indictment, information, or arraignment." In United States v. Gouveia , 467 U.S. 180 (1984) , the Court clarified that an inmate suspected of committing murder while in prison (i.e. murdering another inmate ) lacks the right to counsel while in administrative segregation prior to indictment, because said segregation happens before the "initiation of adversary judicial proceedings." In Moran v. Burbine , 475 U.S. 412, 431 (1986) , the Court found that "a defendant's right to counsel was not violated when the police secured Miranda waivers and interviewed him without informing the defendant that t[he police] had been contacted by an attorney retained without his knowledge by his sister." Moran reinforced the holding in Gouveia by stating that " the first formal charging proceeding [is] the point at which the Sixth Amendment right to counsel initially attaches ." Later in its decision, the Moran court used more open-ended language, holding that the Sixth Amendment "becomes applicable only when the government's role shifts from investigation to accusation. For it is only then that the assistance of one versed in the 'intricacies . . . of law,' is needed to assure that the prosecution's case encounters 'the crucible of meaningful adversarial testing.'" Effective Counsel The Right to Effective Counsel in General In addition, the Supreme Court has ruled that the right to counsel implies the right to an [wex:ineffective assistance of counsel|effective lawyer]. To determine whether a court-appointed attorney has given effective counsel, courts will use the test established by the Supreme Court in Strickland v. Washington , 466 U.S. 668 (1984) . The Court established a two-prong test for whether a court-appointed attorney has given the proper amount of care to a court-appointed client: Error Prong whether counsel's performance is deficient under the circumstances, with performance being measured under the standard of prevailing professional norms this largely relies upon custom Prejudice Prong Whether the lawyer's supposed subpar conduct affected, with reasonable probability, the trial's outcome this relies upon but-for causation : "but for counsel's unprofessional errors, the results of the proceeding would have been different" If the counsel fails this test, then the remedy is to have a new trial . The Conflict Between The Right to Effective Counsel and The Issue of Perjury In Nix v. Whiteside , 475 U.S. 157 (1986) , the Supreme Court found that an attorney in a criminal trial has a duty not to allow client to give perjured information. The ethical duty of an attorney not to allow perjured info supersedes a duty of zealous advocacy. The Supreme Court held that the Sixth Amendment right of a criminal defendant is not violated when an attorney refuses to cooperate with the defendant in presenting perjured evidenc
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of the right to counsel. The original text restricted communication with the media, whereas the changed text broadens this to restrict communication with 'any third party, including legal counsel.' The Law Explanation states that preventing communication with legal counsel infringes upon this right. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating that 'The right to counsel refers to the right of a criminal defendant to have a lawyer assist in his defense, even if he cannot afford to pay for an attorney.' While the snippet focuses on criminal defense, the underlying principle—the right to seek legal assistance—is directly violated by the changed text, creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement with respect to the Confidential Information and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous oral or written agreements concerning such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes a partial agreement with respect to the Confidential Information. FNHA reserves the right to unilaterally modify or supplement this agreement with additional terms and conditions at any time, without prior notice to the Company, and such modifications shall be binding on the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows FNHA to unilaterally modify the agreement without notice, violating basic principles of contract law that require mutual consent for modifications.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law - Mutuality of Agreement
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Contract Law - Consideration and Mutuality
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law requires mutuality of agreement and consideration. Allowing one party to unilaterally modify a contract without the other party's consent undermines the mutuality requirement, potentially rendering the modification unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows FNHA to unilaterally modify the agreement without notice, which contradicts the requirement of mutuality in contract law. The original contract stated it was the 'entire agreement,' but the changed text turns it into a 'partial agreement' where FNHA reserves the right to unilaterally modify it. The Law Explanation specifies that mutuality is undermined when one party can modify a contract without the other's consent, potentially rendering the modification unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable,' and that a modified contract 'requires new consideration.' The unilateral modification clause in the changed text bypasses the requirement for consideration from both parties for modifications, thus creating a contradiction.
